coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots in situ polymerized biodegradable semi-interpenetrating tough hydrogel films with antioxidant and antibacterial activity for applications in food industry
coredata.dc:creator: Alzahrani, Abdulhakeem
coredata.dc:description: A flexible, antioxidant, biodegradable, and UV-resistant polymeric nanocomposite hydrogel with heteroatom-doped carbon dots (CDs) has been fabricated using a simple one-step in situ free radical gelation process. The hydrogel formation and their physico-mehcanical characteristics have been assessed by rheology, uniaxial tensile and compression testing. The water uptake behaviour of the hydrogels is controlled by the CDs by manipulating their internal morphology and porosity. The porous nature of the hydrogels has been found from their scanning electron microscopic images which are also supported by their anomalous diffusion-based transport mechanism. The rheological signatures of the hydrogels show delayed network rupturing due to the secondary physical crosslinking alleviated by CDs. Moreover, CDs are directly influencing the permeabilites (oxygen and moisture) by lowering the values compared to their neat hydrogel films which are essential for a packing material. The biodegradability of the hydrogel films showed gradual weight loss (<75 %) within 3 weeks. The hydrogel films also have been qualified to be acted as antibacterial and antioxidant material. The shelf-life and non-leaching of CDs from gel matrices are also performed which shows its excellent capability to be used as a potential antibacterial, biodegradable, antioxidant alternative packaging material in food sectors.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots in situ polymerized biodegradable semi-interpenetrating tough hydrogel films with antioxidant and antibacterial activity for applications in food industry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science & Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A flexible, antioxidant, biodegradable, and UV-resistant polymeric nanocomposite hydrogel with heteroatom-doped carbon dots (CDs) has been fabricated using a simple one-step in situ free radical gelation process. The hydrogel formation and their physico-mehcanical characteristics have been assessed by rheology, uniaxial tensile and compression testing. The water uptake behaviour of the hydrogels is controlled by the CDs by manipulating their internal morphology and porosity. The porous nature of the hydrogels has been found from their scanning electron microscopic images which are also supported by their anomalous diffusion-based transport mechanism. The rheological signatures of the hydrogels show delayed network rupturing due to the secondary physical crosslinking alleviated by CDs. Moreover, CDs are directly influencing the permeabilites (oxygen and moisture) by lowering the values compared to their neat hydrogel films which are essential for a packing material. The biodegradability of the hydrogel films showed gradual weight loss (<75 %) within 3 weeks. The hydrogel films also have been qualified to be acted as antibacterial and antioxidant material. The shelf-life and non-leaching of CDs from gel matrices are also performed which shows its excellent capability to be used as a potential antibacterial, biodegradable, antioxidant alternative packaging material in food sectors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs or C-dots) have transformed into zero-dimensional nanomaterials in the last decade. Due to their little size (less than 10 nm), they display distinctive fluorescence properties (Kang & Lee, 2019). Furthermore, CDs possess exceptional characteristics such as being soluble in water, having compatibility with living organisms, little toxicity, and the presence of functional groups on their surface (Fu et al., 2022; Mei et al., 2022; Su et al., 2023; Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019). These qualities distinguish CDs from other semiconductor quantum dots that are now accessible in the market. CDs possess adhesion and adsorption capabilities to biological surfaces, rendering them appealing nanomaterials in biomedical domains like as biosensing, bioimaging, bio-targeting, photodynamic treatment (PDT), and drug administration. These properties have already been documented (Bao et al., 2023; Qu, Wang, Bao, & Sun, 2020; Zhao, Chen, Zhao, Gao, Ren, & Qu, 2015). In recent times, CDs have gained significant attention as theragnostic agents for their antioxidant, antibacterial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties. This has led to the exploration of new applications for CDs. Various carbon dots, such as non-metal-doped CDs (containing nitrogen, sulphur, phosphorous) (Chai, Zhou, Chi, Chen, Pei, & Chen, 2022; Ganguly, Das, Itzhaki, Hadad, Gedanken, & Margel, 2020; Piecuch, Obłąk, & Guz-Regner, 2016; Saravanan et al., 2020), green-synthesized CDs (Saravanan, Maruthapandi, Das, Luong, & Gedanken, 2021), and metal-doped CDs (P. Das et al., 2018; Nichols et al., 2020), have been investigated for their potential to combat different infections by exhibiting antibacterial capabilities (P. Das, Ganguly, Margel, & Gedanken, 2021b).

Hydrogels made from renewable resources have recently found use in food storage containers. However, there has been only modest use of superabsorbent polymers as bio-based hydrogels in the food sector (P. Das, Ganguly, Banerjee, & Das, 2019; Ganguly & Margel, 2023; Yang et al., 2021). Industry attention has geared in recent years towards improving the biodegradability, swelling characteristics, tensile and thermal properties of polymers for food packaging applications (P. Das, Ganguly, Banerjee, et al., 2019; Ganguly, Das, Banerjee, & Das, 2019). Hydrogels are polymeric matrices that can soak up liquids up to 100 times their weight without bursting (P. Das, S. Ganguly, et al., 2023; Ganguly & Margel, 2021). In addition, there is a specific type of hydrogel called a superabsorbent hydrogel, which is made up of polymers that can soak up water at a rate of between one thousand and one million times their dry weight (Ganguly, Das, & Das, 2020). There are three main types of packaging: primary packaging, secondary packaging, and tertiary packaging (Akin, Eyduran, & Krauter, 2023). The use of hydrogel sheets in food packaging systems has yielded promising results in recent years (Dai et al., 2021). Bio-based packaging, like traditional packaging, must serve multiple purposes, such as containing and protecting food, keeping it fresh and safe, and informing customers (Sorrentino, Gorrasi, & Vittoria, 2007). The use of a specific biopolymer film, like starch, for packaging is limited because it lacks sufficient mechanical strength. However, it is feasible to improve the interactions between polymers by combining biopolymers with bio- or synthetic polymers that have unique architectures. This can be achieved by predominantly introducing charge interactions instead of relying solely on hydrogen bonding (Mensitieri et al., 2011). Various biopolymer-based hydrogel composites, such as agar (Martínez-Sanz et al., 2020), sodium alginate (Ganguly, Das, Mondal, & Das, 2016; Ganguly, Maity, et al., 2018), carrageenan (Ganguly,Das, et al., 2018), collagen, bacterial cellulose, and CMC, have been investigated for their potential applications in the development of food packaging and preservation films (P. Das, Ganguly, Ahmed, et al., 2022). Incorporating antimicrobial agents or bioactive substances into films is a common practise for improved preservation.

Biopolymers are gaining popularity as a sustainable alternative to petroleum-based food packaging films because they are renewable, biodegradable, safe, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly. Biodegradable packaging materials frequently utilise natural polymers, including proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids, as their primary constituents. Furthermore, these films or coatings derived from biological sources are typically created by combining them together in order to improve their overall properties (Rangaraj, Rambabu, Banat, & Mittal, 2021). Sericin is a hydrophilic macromolecule derived from the Bombyx mori silkworm. Currently, sericin has been discovered to possess a diverse range of biological functionalities. It has strong reactivity and exceptional biological properties, including biodegradability, biocompatibility, antibacterial efficacy, anti-oxidation capabilities, and resistance to UV radiation. Additionally, it demonstrates high solubility and release rates in aqueous environments (Y.-Q. Zhang, 2002). Natural polysaccharides (cellulose, starch, sodium alginate, pectin, chitosan, etc.), proteins (soy protein, animal protein), and lipids (shellac resin, acetyl glycerides, waxes, etc.) films are used for food packaging (Amin et al., 2021; P. Das, Ganguly, Ahmed, et al., 2022; T. K. Das & Ganguly, 2023). Chitosan (CS) is a polysaccharide derived from chitin by the deacetylation process. It exhibits multiple favourable characteristics, such as non-toxicity, biodegradability, and antibacterial properties. The interaction between the positively charged CS molecule and the negatively charged microbial cell membrane can lead to the expulsion of internal components of bacteria (J. Li & Zhuang, 2020). Since CS can generate synergistic effects when combined with other compounds, it has several potential applications as an active biological material. CS is a unique cationic polysaccharide derived from natural sources. It exhibits a range of significant bioactivities, including antioxidant properties (such as scavenging free radicals, chelating metal ions, regulating antioxidant enzyme activities, and reducing lipid peroxidation). Additionally, CS demonstrates antitumor, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, immunoregulatory, anti-obesity, and hypocholesterolemic activities (Ivanova & Yaneva, 2020). CS exhibits advantageous characteristics in terms of biocompatibility, biodegradability, low immunogenicity, and nontoxicity. Furthermore, there have been numerous studies documenting the adsorption of heavy metal ions, moisture retention, and film forming properties of CS. Therefore, CS has numerous uses in the food, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, biological, environmental, and engineering sectors (Baranwal et al., 2018).

This article describes the synthesis of a luminescent chitosan-based semi-interpenetrating polymer network (IPN) hydrogel using in situ free radical gelation. The work was conducted in two sequential stages. Initially, fluorescent carbon dots were synthesised, and subsequently, they were integrated into the hydrogel matrix. The synthesis of fluorescent hydrogel was confirmed by FTIR spectrum. Moreover, the physical characteristics of the hydrogels were tested by its rheological and mechanical testing. The hydrogel showed highly elastic and zero permanent set behavior in both uniaxial stretching and compression test. The antibacterial and antioxidant behavior could promote this material as a food packaging and preservation material.

Acrylamide (AM) and 2-aminoethyl methacrylate (AEM) were purchased from Merck and Sigma Aldrich respectively and used as received. N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), redox initiator couple, i.e., potassium peroxidisulfate (K2S2O8) and sodium metabisulfide (Na2S2O5) were also purchased from Merck. Chitosan (medium molecular weight, 190–310 kDa; deacetylated 75 %+) was procured from Merck, Germany. All reagents used here are of analytical grades. Hen egg white lysozyme hydrochloride (from TCI Pvt. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used as received. Double distilled water (DW) was used for experimental works.

CDs were prepared by thermal coupling of polyethylenimine (PEI) and l-cysteine. Initially 0.5 g of PEI was dissolved in 8 mL of distilled water. After that, 0.2 g of l-cysteine in 7 mL solution was mixed to the PEI solution and stirred for 1 h. The solution was then transferred to a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 15 min. After the reaction, a sticky brownish product was obtained. The solid mass was dissolved using distilled water. The supernatant was collected subsequent to centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The solution was then filtered through a 0.22 µm pore size membrane to remove any large particles. Finally, the solution was dialyzed (MWCO 500–100 kDa) and kept at 4 °C for subsequent investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent chitosan/poly(acrylamide-co-2-aminoethyl methacrylate) hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan/poly(AM-co-AEM) was prepared via free radical polymerization approach under inert atmosphere using potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) and sodium metabisulphite (Na2S2O5) as redox initiator and MBA as crosslinker. At first 0.5 % chitosan was dissolved by slow addition in 1 % acetic acid solution followed by monomer addition. Next, AM and AEM monomers were taken 1:1 (weight ratio) and poured into the dissolved chitosan system. The total monomer concentration was kept 20 % with respect to the total volume. After completion of the homogenisation, specific amount of CDs dispersion was added into the solution mixture and stirred for 15 min. Then the whole prepolymer mixture was transferred into cylindrical glass mold and kept sealed. Then, the whole system was put into a water bath maintaining the temperature in between 60 and 65 °C throughout the process. After the gelation, the gel was immediately removed from the mold and disintegrated into required dimension blocks. The blocks were then immersed into 70 % ethanol aqueous solution for 24 h to remove water soluble oligomeric fractions, unreacted monomers and initiator residues.

Preweighted xerogel specimens (10 x 10 x 0.1 mm) were tested by being submerged in a 1.5 g/mL concentrated (2 mL in volume) lysozyme solution at phosphate buffer solution (pH 7). All trials were conducted at a constant 37 degrees Celsius. After removing the samples from the solution and washing them with distilled water, they were weighed. In order to maintain a steady enzyme activity, the lysozyme solution was replaced at regular intervals.

A known weight of hydrogel specimen was dipped in distilled water at ambient temperature until equilibrium reached. During this process the swelled hydrogel was weighted at different intervals after removal of loosely attached surface water with filter paper. The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated as per the equations given below:
(1)

DPPH was used to assess the free radical scavenging capabilities of various concentrations of CDs. In brief, various amounts of CDs were added to 100 µM methanolic solution of 1 mL DPPH. The resulting mixtures were incubated in the dark for 30 min after that the absorbance at 517 nm was measured. The DPPH free radical scavenging was measured as follows:
(2)
%
scavenging activity
=
(
A c
-
A s
)
/
A c
where Ac and As denote the absorbance of DPPH free radicals at 517 nm in the absence and presence of CDs.

The ABTS radical cation decolorization test was used to determine the free radical scavenging activity. 5 mL of 7 mM aqueous ABTS solution and 88 µL of 140 mM (2.45 mM final concentration) potassium persulfate solution were used to make the ABTS solution. The radical cation solution was diluted to an initial absorbance value of 0.7 ± 0.05 at 734 nm after 16 h of storage in the dark. After that, the dispersion was treated with various doses of CDs, and the absorbance was recorded. The antioxidant activity of ABTS was estimated using the formula:
(3)
%
scavenging activity
= ( Ac - As ) / Ac
where Ac and As denote the absorbance at 734 nm in the absence and presence of CDs.

The MTT assay is a colorimetric method used to evaluate cell viability by measuring cellular metabolic activity. The impact of the CHCDs was investigated by utilising human epithelial cells derived from the eye, which were cultivated on 96-well cell culture plates. Following the administration of CHCDs for duration of 1–5 days, an MTT assay was performed in accordance with the guidelines provided by the manufacturer to ascertain the proportion of viable cells. The cell viability of the untreated cells (control) was measured at 100 %. All results are reported as mean values ± standard deviation (n = 3).

The extent of CDs released from the hydrogel films was estimated using food simulant solutions. For this, the film sample (2 cm × 2 cm) was added to a 100 mL conical flask containing 20 mL of different food simulant solutions (10 %, 50 %, and 95 % ethanol, and water for simulating alcoholic, and aqueous food, respectively) and kept at 25 °C with gentle shaking. 1 mL of the solution was taken at predetermined time intervals and measured the absorbance at 295 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (P. Das, Ganguly, Ahmed, et al., 2022).

All tests were performed in triplicate, and the experimental results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and differences (p < 0.05) between individual means were analyzed by Duncan’s multiple range test utilizing the SPSS statistical analysis computer program (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The CDs were prepared by a simple one step thermal coupling approach. The optical characteristics of the CDs samples were assessed using ultraviolet (UV) and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. In Fig. 1 a, it was observed that the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs exhibit absorption peak centered at around 356 nm which is attributed to the n-π* transition of the CN, and C═O bonds(P. Das, M. Sherazee, et al., 2023). The inset depicts the aqueous solution of the synthesised CDs, exhibiting a yellowish colour when exposed to daylight and a bluish-green colour when illuminated with UV light. The fluorescence characteristics of the CDs were investigated by varying the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 1b. The excitation independent emission behaviour is observed when the excitation wavelength was varied from 320 to 420 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1b. Initially, the fluorescence intensity exhibits an increasing pattern in response to excitation, reaching its peak at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Afterwards, a subsequent increment in the excitation wavelength leads to a progressive reduction in the fluorescence signal without any impact on the peak position. The highest fluorescence emission was measured at 457 nm with a Stoke shift of 122 nm at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The excitation-independent photoluminescence feature is attributed to the narrow particle size distribution and lesser amount of defect states within the quantum dots(P. Das, Ganguly, Bose, et al., 2019). The impact of storage time on the fluorescence response of the CDs was also evaluated, and it was discovered that there was no appreciable change (p > 0.05) in the fluorescence signal of the CDs (Fig. 1c). These findings suggest that the as-prepared CDs have good photostability, making them acceptable for use in a variety of applications. We used TEM characterization to study the morphology of the synthesized CDs. The as-prepared CDs, as seen in Fig. S1a, had spherical morphology and were distributed across the section at room temperature. The sizes of the CDs are between 3 and 8 nm. In order to have an insight of the composition and chemical characteristics of the CDs, we performed XPS and FTIR studies. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1d) showsfour distinct peaks at 168.1 eV, 284.9 eV, 399.7 eV, 532.3 eV indicating the existence of S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The result indicates the existence of the elements in the CDs. The high resolution C1s spectrum reveals three peaks as depicted in Fig. S1b. The peak at about 282.9 eV and 285.0 eV suggests the presence of CC and CO, CS, and CN, respectively. Another peak around 288.9 eV could be assigned to CO. The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. S1c) show the existence of pyridinic (399.5 eV) and pyrrolic (400.5 eV) N atoms(Dong et al., 2013). The high resolution O1s spectra (Fig. S1d) unveils the peaks at 532.3 and 533.5 eV are ascribed to the binding energies of CO and CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of S2p (Fig. S1e) demonstrate the presence of CSC units(Choi, Chung, Park, & Woo, 2013; Dong et al., 2013). Fig. 1e shows the FTIR spectrum of the CDs. The broadpeak at 3434 cm−1 in the FTIRspectra can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH or NH bonds(P. Das, Ganguly, Saha, Noked, Margel, & Gedanken, 2021). The stretching vibrations of the CN or CS bonds, NH, –COO, and CN groups are responsible for the additional peaks seen at 1112 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1, respectively(Luo et al., 2018). Two absorption peaks corresponding to the amide I and amide II bands are seen at 1650 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, respectively(Bourlinos, Zbořil, Petr, Bakandritsos, Krysmann, & Giannelis, 2012). The bands at 2942 and 2837 cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of single –CH bonds, respectively(Atchudan, Edison, Chakradhar, Perumal, Shim, & Lee, 2017).

The current study employed DPPH radicals and ABTS radicals to assess the antioxidant characteristics of CDs. The DPPH free radical is commonly employed as a means to investigate the antioxidant capacity of various substances(P. Das, Ganguly, Margel, & Gedanken, 2021a). Hence, the assessment of the antioxidant activity of CDs was conducted by the utilisation of the DPPH free radical scavenging test. The utilisation of a DPPH radical is frequently applied in the assessment of antioxidative activity, with a focus on the spectrophotometric alterations resulting from the interaction between DPPH• and an antioxidant. This approach is characterised by its speed, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness. DPPH can be classified as a stable free radical due to the presence of nitrogen atoms with unpaired electrons and its arrangement inside a molecular structure consisting of three benzene rings. The interaction between DPPH and an antioxidant involves the acceptance of a hydrogen radical, resulting in the formation of a DPPH–H complex. This transformation is accompanied by a change in colour from deep violet to pale yellow (Pyrzynska & Pękal, 2013). The evaluation of the reduction of the DPPH radical can be conducted by measuring the absorbance at 517 nm relative to a blank sample. The investigation of dose-dependent DPPH radical scavenging ability by CDs was conducted within a concentration range of 0 to 600 μg/mL, as depicted in Fig. 2 a. The study observed an increase in the DPPH scavenging activity as the concentration of CDs was incrementally raised up to 500 μg/mL (p < 0.05). Subsequent increments of CDs result in a marginal alteration in the antioxidative efficacy, ultimately attaining a state of saturation at around 600 μg/mL (p > 0.05). The concentration-dependent ABTS•+ scavenging activities of CDs were observed, as shown by the colour change from blue-green to colourless (Fig. 2b). The decrease in the absorbance value was confirmed as the concentration of CDs increased gradually from 0 to 700 μg/mL. The scavenging results of ABTS•+ exhibited a dose-dependent relationship, wherein the scavenging activity increased with increasing concentrations of CDs up to 400 μg/mL shows significant changes (p < 0.05), after which saturation was observed in the range of 400–700 μg/mL (p > 0.05). The inclusion of dopants can potentially enhance the process of radical scavenging by facilitating the transfer of electrons to radicals, subsequently leading to either proton transfer or the generation of an adduct(P. Das, Ganguly, Ahmed, et al., 2022). The hydrogen transfer from the hydroxyl groups of CDs has the ability to decrease and quench the free radicals ABTS• and DPPH•. Fig. 2c illustrates the comprehensive representation of the radical scavenging activity of CDs(Ruiz, Yate, García, Cabanero, & Grande, 2017).

The hydrogel was synthesised using the process of free radical graft copolymerization, in which acrylamide (AM) and 2-aminoethyl methacrylate (AEM) were combined with chitosan in the presence of the MBA crosslinker. Potassium persulfate and sodium metabisulfite were used as redox initiators, which work at a lower temperature than thermal initiators. Low temperature reactions tend to make longer chains, which makes the hydrogel stronger (Ganguly, Maity, et al., 2018; Ganguly, Maity, Mondal, Das, & Das, 2017). In Fig. 3 a, the suggested process of grafting and gel formation was explained. A Michael-type reaction describes the grafting phenomenon. Alkoxy radicals form on the chitosan main chain backbone when free radicals remove hydrogen from the hydroxyl group. An inherently or in situ activated form of chitosan is referred to as a chitosan macro-initiator. When the chitosan macro-radical reaches the vinylic site, it accepts the radical and the graft polymerization process can begin. The monomers' functional groups are generated via homolytic cleavage of more reactive vinyl groups. On the other hand, starch macro-initiators are highly reactive and can form covalent bonds with activated vinylic monomers. MBA acts as a bifunctional monomer which reacts simultaneously with the growing radicals and a crosslinked insoluble mass was produced. Fig. 3(b-d) shows the unfilled/pristine hydrogel in white light. Fig. 3(e-g) shows CDs loaded nanocomposite hydrogels in white light. Both the hydrogels are also irradiated under UV light (365 nm) and shown in Fig. 3(h,i). The figure shows that CDs loaded hydrogel have very prominent blue fluorescent. The gel time determination was also measured as shown in Fig. S1f. For the unfilled hydrogel the gel time as ∼ 11 min and for 0.2 wt% CDs loaded hydrogel the gel time was ∼ 9 min. but with increasing the CDs concentration the gel time as decreased to 2 min. this could be inferred to the gelation promoter behavior of CDs (Bhattacharya et al., 2019).

The FTIR analysis confirmed evidence of hydrogel formation. The absorption bands associated with CO stretching at 1740 cm−1 (ester groups) and CO bending at 1646 cm−1 (ester) were observed in the FTIR spectra of the CHCD0 (Fig. 4 a)(Esselin, Portolan, Domloge, Rees, Musa, & Pilard, 2016). The peaks at 1393 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 were attributed to CN stretching and NH2 groups, respectively(Esselin et al., 2016). The observed peak at 3220 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the –NH2 and –OH functional groups. This peak is sharperin the CHCD2, indicating that hydrogen bonding is higher. The intensity of the bands in the region of 2942–2837 cm−1 (asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of single –CH bonds) were also increased(Atchudan et al., 2017). The stretching vibrations of the CN or CS bonds, NH, –COO, and CN groups are responsible for the additional peaks seen at 1114 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1, respectively. The polymerisation and gelation could also be confined to assess the FTIR spectra of vinylic (CC) of the hydrogel specimen. The characteristic peaks of vinylic double bonds are ∼ 1146 and ∼ 949 cm−1; but these peaks are absent in case of CHCD hydrogels. Thus could infer the diminishing of vinylic bond resulting in free radical polymerisation (Ganguly, Das, Das, et al., 2020; Mali, Dhawale, & Dias, 2017).

The basic requirement for hydrogel characterizations is the swelling experiment, which reveals the hydrogel's water uptake behaviour in different solvents. Different reaction variables were used to generate a variety of hydrogels, all of which were then subjected to the swelling process. Swelling effects are depicted graphically in Fig. 4b,c by the equilibrium swelling ratio (ESR) in water. The water uptake behavior of hydrogels is significantly changed when in cooperation with carbon dots are varied. For unfilled hydrogel the swell ratio is almost 9.5. Upon the addition of carbon dots into the gel matrix, the absorption of water was limited, resulting in a decrease in the swell ratio to 6.2. Impact of CDs in water uptake is significant during the swelling period (p < 0.05). This phenomenon can be explained by the development of broad secondary connections between the polarities on the surface of the carbon dots and the additional groups attached to the hydrogel polymer chains. The time-dependent water infiltration has been found for Fig. 4b. For each and every hydrogel, the graph displays a progressive increase in weight over the course of time, which is indicative of an increase in the volume of the hydrogels. When it comes to CDs loaded hydrogel (0.2 wt%), the swelling ratio and the time-dependent water uptake are essentially identical to those of the pristine hydrogel. However, when the concentration of CDs was raised, it resulted in a reduction in water absorption, indicating the creation of a significant number of crosslinks within the gel matrix.

Hydrogels were analysed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to compare their morphology with and without CD loading. The CDs-loaded hydrogels (Fig. 4e-g) had a more porous morphology than the unloaded pure hydrogel CHCD0 (Fig. 4d). The creation of a dense and compact network in hydrogels was seen as pore diameters shrank with increasing CDs loading (P. Das, Ganguly, Saravanan, et al., 2022). For CDs-reinforced hydrogels with varying loadings, the water imbibition rate into the hydrogel matrix is affected by the density of the network. The pore dimension of the hydrogels was lowered with incorporation of CDs into the hydrogel matrix. The swelling data also supported such kind of behavior where high CDs containing hydrogels showed less water uptake compared to the pristine/unfilled hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological analysis of the hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogels in their swollen state may experience a deficiency in mechanical strength. In order to examine the gel strength and hardness of the swelled hydrogels, rheological experiments were conducted. The rheological properties were evaluated in a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. The frequency sweep test of the samples is illustrated in Fig. 4h, displaying the elastic moduli of the hydrogels. The data clearly indicates that the elastic moduli of the hydrogels are positively correlated with the concentration of CDs within the gel matrix. This indicates the ability of CDs to enhance and fortify materials as nanofillers. For evaluating the gel strength, the hydrogels’ elastic to viscous moduli ratio
G ′
/
G ″
have been calculated and plotted in Fig. 4i. The gel strength shows a gradual increment in the ratio
G ′
/
G ″
with CDs content. The ratio signifies actually the dominance of elastic part over the viscous part. This also supports the rheology data. CDs are actually not only simply entrapped into the polymer phase, but also the polymer chains are adsorbed on to the surface of CDs forming physical adhesion between nanoparticles and macrochains. Moreover, the CDs also showed the effect of network strengthening. Fig. 4j is the stress sweep experiment which shows the change of elastic moduli of the hydrogels at variable stress. It could be seen that the pure (unfilled) hydrogel has an onset of sudden lowering of stress value at ∼ 800 Pa. but after addition of CDs, the stress value right shifted which could be effect of CDs-polymer chain interactions and intermolecular H-bonding. The abrupt decrease in elastic modulus typically occurs as a result of gel network rupture. This could be referred to as a 'crucial juncture of network disruption'. However, the introduction of CDs has caused a shift to the right in the gel network rupturing, indicating a delay in the network rupturing process due to the presence of CDs. CDs facilitate the formation of more physical crosslinking points within the matrix, leading to an increase in elastic moduli.

Mechanical properties of prepared hydrogel films are evaluated against both the uniaxial tension and compressive forces because both the forces are operated for a packaging material. Fig. 5 a is showing the uniaxial tensile stress vs. strain plot of the pristine and CDs filled hydrogel films. All are showing little bit of elastomeric nature in their plot. After incorporation of CDs the characteristic remains identical to the pristine one but the ultimate tensile strength was increased. The elongation at break of the hydrogel (pristine/unfiled) was around 360 % which was decreased after incorporation of CDs, which was statistically significant (p < 0.05). This implied enhancement of elasticity of the CDs filled hydrogel films. The cyclic loading–unloading curve of the hydrogel films were also assessed as shown in Fig. 5b. The cyclic stress–strain plot shows hysteresis but the hysteresis loss was minimized with CDs content. The unloading cycle shows almost zero point merging which could imply its negligible permanent set behavior. This could be termed as elastomeric nature of the hydrogel films. The compression tests of the hydrogels were also evaluated against uniaxial compressive stress as shown in Fig. 5c. The compression stress–strain behavior shows ‘J’-type trend for all the hydrogels. The CDs incorporated films are also showing increase in compression strength as like tensile test. The cyclic compression stress–strain plot (Fig. 5d) shows similar to tensile plot where no permanent/residual strain was o0nbserved. This could be attributing to their highly elastic nature. CDs are actually distributed throughout the polymer gel matrices and the polymer macrochains are physisorped on to surfaces of CDs. This CDs are acting like stress dissipation centers. More the CDs inside the matrix, more feasible would be the stress transfer resulting better elasticity. The low temperature withstanding capability of food packaging films are also necessary to address. Herein, we also studied the hybrid films in different temperature and compare them with the ambient conditions (28 °C). the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of the hybrid CDs loaded films were kept at ∼ 12 °C in a closed container and keeping the relative humidity (RH) > 90 %. At low temperature the polyelectrolyte chains start to freeze because of their high glass transition temperature (>ambient temperature). But for hydrogel films the water molecules act as plasticizer resulting better flexibility at high RH values (Hodge, Bastow, Edward, Simon, & Hill, 1996; L. Li, Guan, Yang, Qin, & Feng, 2018; Quinn, Kampff, Smyth, & McBrierty, 1988). Thus, the ductility and the UTS were not drastically changed compared to the pre-cool state. For ensuring their robustness at low temperature, all the films were kept for 1–5 h span and took out for tensile testing. The UTS values have been showed in the histogram plot at Figure S2.

Physiological metabolism in fruits and vegetables is controlled by gaseous chemicals present in packaging and responsible for most decay mechanisms. Fruits and vegetables were able to preserve longer because the optimal gas condition (low oxygen and high carbon dioxide) reduced their respiratory impact and prevented the growth of microbes (Moradi, Molaei, Kousheh, T. Guimarães, & McClements, 2023). Fig. 5e and 5f present the oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of prisitine and CDs loaded hydrogel films as a function of the CDs content. The OTR and WVTR of the unfilled hydrogel films were around 65 cm3.μm/(m2.d.kPa) and WVTR value of 877 g/m2.24 h; respectively. After incorporation of CDs, both the OTR and WVTR were decreasing. The CHCD1 specimen exhibits negligible changes in OTR (oxygen transmission rate) and WVTR (water vapour transmission rate) as compared to the pristine hydrogel film (CHC0); nevertheless, the variation was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). However, the reduction in oxygen transmission rate (OTR) and water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) was more noticeable (p < 0.05) in the films made with greater concentration CDs hydrogel, as depicted in the corresponding figures. For CHCD3 specimen the OTR and WVTR were evaluated to ∼ 40 cm3.μm/(m2.d.kPa) and ∼ 637 g/m2.24 h; respectively. Hydrogel films exhibited enhanced gas barrier properties due to their more compact network structures, which hindered the movement of the polymer matrix associated with the gaseous diffusion coefficient (Wang, Yuan, Tian, Zhang, & Lu, 2019). The permeability of gas is closely correlated with the level of interconnectivity inside the hydrogel. CDs, being physical crosslinking systems, might lead to reduced gas permeability due to the presence of dense polymer matrices. Hydrogel films were characterised for their UV-blocking performance, and their UV–vis transmittance spectra are displayed in Fig. 5g. The film's composition clearly had an effect on the amount of light that passed through it. The CHCD0 film had the poorest UV-blocking performance, since its greatest transmittance was found in that range. Transmittance of approximately 0 % in the complete UV region by CHCD3 hydrogel film after CDs doping indicated excellent UV-shielding capacity (p < 0.05). The possible explanation for this phenomenon: increased transmittance of CHCD3 at 400 nm confirms that the auxochromic group of improved the UV shielding capacity of the hydrogel films.

Disposal of polymeric materials after their usage is completed is an ongoing problem in public life and an environmental hazard. Therefore, it is crucial in academic sectors to promote the synthesised materials in practical applications by testing the biodegradation assay of the produced hydrogels. The results of the CDs containing hydrogel biodegradation investigation are depicted in Fig. 5h. The rate of degradation was measured at the beginning and at 3, 7, and 21 days which implies significant weight loss of film during lysozyme assisted degradation (p < 0.05). Lysozyme isolated from chicken eggs is structurally similar to human lysozyme (Ganguly et al., 2017). Degradation without lysozyme was also compared using phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Polysaccharide digestion is aided by lysozyme, a glycoside hydrolase that cleaves the 1, 4-beta glycosidic link (Freier, Koh, Kazazian, & Shoichet, 2005). According to the results of the biodegradation investigation, the mass of hydrogels made from cross-linked starch steadily declines over time. The enzymatic cleavage of the starch (a polysaccharide) backbone chain yields a glucopyranose type ring of sugar, which accounts for this phenomenon (Ganguly & Das, 2018). When the degradation rate drops, it's because the number of degradation sites has decreased (B. Zhang et al., 2015). Hydrogel samples immersed in PBS exhibited more swelling behaviour than degradation, which is noteworthy (p > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of the composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant activity of the CDs loaded hybrid hydrogel was evaluated by DPPH and ABTS assay. The different antioxidant properties of hydrogels integrated with CDs are dependent on the concentration of CDs. CDs are widely acknowledged as efficacious antioxidants, with their effectiveness depending upon their origin and the way of preparation. In both the ABTS and DPPH tests, the CHCD0 sample had less than 3 % free radical scavenger activity. However, the introduction of CDs resulted in an increase of the DPPH and ABTS scavenging activity exhibited by the composite hydrogels (Fig. 6 a,b) which is significant compared to the control group (p < 0.05). It is generally believed that the high antioxidant activity of CDs is due, in part, to the abundance of electron-donating functionalities at the surface of the CDs. These functionalities include hydroxyl, carboxyl, and others. However, the insertion of CDs resulted in a composite hydrogel with stronger antioxidant activity than the pure CHCD0, which indicates that the free radical scavenging function was greatly increased by the addition of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of the composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The functional feature of antimicrobial activity is extensively employed in many active food packaging technologies. The assessment of the antibacterial efficacy of CDs based hydrogels was conducted against food-borne pathogenic bacteria L. monocytogenes and E. coli as depicted in Fig. 6c,d. As chitosan itself demonstrated antibacterial activity(Priyadarshi, Kumar, Deeba, Kulshreshtha, & Negi, 2018) indicating a notable bacteriostatic impact, which can be attributed to the inherent antimicrobial properties of CDs unloaded hydrogel. Nevertheless, the incorporation of CDs into the hydrogels resulted in a noteworthy enhancement (p < 0.05) of antibacterial activity against E. coli and L. monocytogenes which can be seen in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, respectively. Freshly grown E. coli and L. monocytogenes cells were treated separately with the CHCD3 hydrogel and incubated at 37 °C under shaking condition for 24 h. At the end of incubation, 100 μL of the culture was diluted and spread over the nutrient agar plate and further incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in static condition. The bacterial cells incubated with the CHCD3 hydrogel indicated that this hydrogel is efficient to inhibit notable bacterial growth, whereas the control plates for both types of bacteria show significant bacterial growth (p < 0.05) (Figure S3). The findings align with prior research indicating that polymeric films incorporating sulphur nanoparticles or sulphur doped quantum dots exhibit strong antibacterial properties against L. monocytogenes and E. coli (Priyadarshi, Kim, & Rhim, 2021).

The antibacterial properties, antioxidant features of the CDs loaded hydrogels encouraged us to investigate their potential applications in food monitoring. The as-synthesized hydrogel films have good transparency, antibacterial and antioxidant properties, thereby applied for extending the shelf life of foods. Hydrogel films were used to wrap banana and photographs were captured at regular time intervals during the duration of storage (Fig. 6e). On the initial day, bananas exhibited golden yellow colour and hadflawless exteriors, as depicted in Fig. 6e. However, throughout the course of subsequent days in storage, they underwent ripening and ultimately rotted. This can be demonstrated by the identification of darkened patches and spots. The banana coated with CDs based hydrogel revealed a lower level of deterioration compared to the uncoated one. The findings demonstrate that the composite hydrogel film exhibited a good antioxidant capability, hence offering potential for enhancing fruit preservation and extending its duration of shelf life.

Figure S4 illustrates the growth and changes in population of human epithelial cells in an artificial environment after 1st, 3rd, and 5th day on the hydrogel surface and tissue culture plate (TCP). The cell viability was evaluated by assessing the metabolic activity of cells. The MTT assay of the hydrogel disc specimen revealed a cell count of approximately 4.78 ± 0.49 × 105 and 5.19 ± 0.57 × 105 for the control and CHCD3 nanocomposite hydrogel disc, respectively, on the 5th day of the study. This presents data regarding the increased cellular density within the gel matrix.

The release of CDs from the hydrogel films was quantified using food simulant fluids, and the results are illustrated in Figure S5(a-c) for the CHCD samples. The release of CDs from the hybrid films was dependent upon the polarity of the food simulant media. During the release investigation, four different food simulant systems were examined: water, 10 % ethanol, 50 % ethanol, and 95 % ethanol. In the aqueous environment, the release of the entrapped CDs from the films was rapid and exceeded 90 %. The burst release was evident in the water-based release kinetics, but the release became more regulated as the relative water content in the food simulant solutions decreased. For CHCD3, the burst release was more rigorous than CHCD1 because of the significant concentration gradient of CDs between the film and media. The release kinetics in both cases exhibited a deceleration as the relative water content was reduced. This may be due to a discrepancy in polarity between the environment and CDs. Upon immersing the films into the food simulants, there was a process of tiny molecular diffusion, specifically of water and alcohol. As a result of this diffusion mechanism, CDs were released from the polymer system. To evaluate the manner of release mechanism, the first release behaviour (first approximately 2 h) was recorded for both the films. Figure S5(d-f) clearly demonstrates that the release kinetics adheres to the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, specifically the power-law model. The model for fitting is as below;
F
D
=
m t
m α
=
K
K P
t
n
The release of CDs at time “t” and at equilibrium is represented by the variables mt and mα, respectively. The Korsmeyer–Peppas constant, represented by KKP, and the diffusion coefficient, represented by “n”, provide insight into the release mechanism of CDs. The information was collected in triplicate, and it is accompanied by a confidence interval of 95 %. The generated “n” parameter for the release had a computed value below 0.5, confirming the structural morphology of the matrix phase, which is a feature of non-swellable hard spheres. The diffusion exponent (n value) is below 0.5, indicating the presence of a non-Fickian anomalous transport mechanism for CDs from the hybrid films. The initial rapid release occurred as a result of the concentration gradient. However, after a specific duration, the CDs reached a condition of equilibrium and remained constant after 7 h. The KKP values of both films reduced from 1.02 to 0.977 when the polarity was altered from pure water to a 95 % ethanol food simulant. These data indicate that the rate of diffusion is directly correlated with the polarity of the mechanism for CDs release. Upon reducing the polarity, the KKP values exhibited a progressive reduction in both hybrid films. The encapsulation efficiency is an additional parameter that can reflect the extent to which CDs are trapped within the films when they come into contact with food simulants. As previously stated, reducing the polarity of the simulants resulted in a greater trapping of CDs compared to a pure water environment. Fig. S5(g) displays the histograms illustrating the comparative entrapment efficiencies of the CHCD1, CHCD2 and CHCD3 specimens. Consequently, the hybrid films exhibited a higher degree of control over the leaching of CDs when exposed to 95 % ethanol, as opposed to the simulants with lower alcohol content. This might be attributed to the greater solubility of CDs in media that are rich in water. In a water-rich environment, molecular diffusion is more pronounced than in a highly alcoholic environment. The release of CD in a 4 % acetic solution exhibited minimal delay in its release kinetics when compared to pure water. When exposed to an acidic environment, polymer chains get protonated, resulting in a more compact structure. This could potentially restrict the release of CDs from the gel matrix. Furthermore, CDs also exhibit an inclination for in situ gelation inside a polyelectrolytic environment. Therefore, in CDs loaded specimens, the release was somewhat lower when compared to a pure water environment.

In this work, a carbon dots impregnated hydrogel has been fabricated by free radical in situ gelation technique. The carbon dots synthesis was confirmed by their UV, PL, XPS, and FTIR analysis. The hydrogel’s water uptake behavior was studied and showed exponential increment with time. The mechanical properties of the hydrogels in both tension and compression modes showed its tough and elastomeric nature without any permanent set behavior. Moreover, the porous nature of the hydrogel is also obtained from electron microscopic analysis. The antibacterial behavior of the hydrogel was reported which was due to the presence of antibacterial carbon dots. The UV protection of the hydrogel films showed restriction of UVA and UVB and this behavior was more prominent with increasing the CDs concentration into the gel matrices. The antioxidant behavior of the hydrogel is also performed which show that the CDs impregnated hydrogels are antioxidant in both the DPPH and ABTS experiments. The antioxidant behavior is also increased with increasing the CDs content. Lastly, for practical applications, banana is coated with the CDs containing hydrogel thin films and showed at least 5 days storage life without deteriorating their appearance. These all testing results could promote this material for an ideal alternative in food sector and packaging industry.
Abdulhakeem Alzahrani: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
